stage.name: Six-stage map: draw the trunk before details
stage.description: A source-grounded architecture-spine lesson that establishes the CV32A60X six-stage pipeline before learners dive into individual modules.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

content.title: Six-stage map: draw the trunk before details
content.subtitle: Before reading individual RTL modules, anchor the CVA6 architecture around the official six-stage pipeline.
content.thesis: A clean CVA6 reading path starts with the pipeline trunk, then attaches hazards, prediction, exceptions, and memory behavior to the right place.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: global pipeline overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

content.speakerNotes: Welcome to Unit 03. This unit is the architecture reading spine. The goal is not to memorize every internal signal. The goal is to place each module on the main path so later details have somewhere to attach. For CV32A60X, the official design document gives us a six-stage trunk: PC generation, fetch, decode, issue, execute, and commit.

actions.text: Welcome to Unit 03. This unit is the architecture reading spine. The goal is not to memorize every internal signal. The goal is to place each module on the main path so later details have somewhere to attach. For CV32A60X, the official design document gives us a six-stage trunk: PC generation, fetch, decode, issue, execute, and commit.

scenes.title: The official block diagram is a map, not decoration

content.title: Read the big boxes first
content.subtitle: FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT, CONTROLLER, and CSRFILE form the first architecture map.

callouts.title: DECODE / ISSUE
callouts.body: Turn fetched instructions into tracked backend work.

callouts.title: EXECUTE / COMMIT
callouts.body: Produce results, then retire them into architectural state.

content.takeaway: Do not start with a random RTL file. Start with the map the design doc gives you.

assets.alt: Official CVA6 architecture overview block diagram
assets.caption: Official CVA6 architecture overview diagram from the CV32A60X design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: architecture and modules
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The architecture block diagram groups FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT, CONTROLLER, and CSRFILE as the core reading map.

content.speakerNotes: The architecture diagram is not a pretty picture we paste into a slide and ignore. It is the first reading contract. The big boxes tell us which responsibilities exist and where to look next. FRONTEND owns PC generation and fetch. DECODE and ISSUE translate instructions into backend work. EXECUTE produces results. COMMIT decides what becomes architectural state.

actions.text: The architecture diagram is not a pretty picture we paste into a slide and ignore. It is the first reading contract. The big boxes tell us which responsibilities exist and where to look next. FRONTEND owns PC generation and fetch. DECODE and ISSUE translate instructions into backend work. EXECUTE produces results. COMMIT decides what becomes architectural state.

scenes.title: The trunk: six stages in order

content.title: Read from next-PC decision to retirement
content.subtitle: The design document names the main order. Keep this order in your head before adding side paths.

steps.title: PC Generation
steps.body: Choose the next fetch address using sequential flow, prediction, or redirects.

steps.title: Instruction Fetch
steps.body: Request instruction data, realign it, and feed the instruction queue.

steps.title: Instruction Decode
steps.body: Expand and decode instructions into information the backend can track.

steps.title: Issue
steps.body: Check hazards, operands, and readiness before sending work to execute.

steps.title: Execute
steps.body: Run functional units and produce results, branch decisions, or memory requests.

steps.title: Commit
steps.body: Retire results in order and update architectural state precisely.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: global pipeline overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the trunk. PC generation decides where the next instruction should come from. Fetch brings instruction bytes into a usable queue. Decode turns instruction bits into backend meaning. Issue checks whether the instruction is safe to send forward. Execute produces the actual result or control decision. Commit retires the instruction in order. The names are simple; the discipline is keeping them in order.

actions.text: Here is the trunk. PC generation decides where the next instruction should come from. Fetch brings instruction bytes into a usable queue. Decode turns instruction bits into backend meaning. Issue checks whether the instruction is safe to send forward. Execute produces the actual result or control decision. Commit retires the instruction in order. The names are simple; the discipline is keeping them in order.

scenes.title: In-order issue, out-of-order results, in-order commit

content.title: Do not flatten the execution model
content.subtitle: CVA6 is single issue and issues instructions in order, but backend results can complete out of order before commit restores architectural order.

columns.title: Front of backend
columns.body: The issue path preserves program order when admitting instructions into execution.

columns.title: Back of backend
columns.body: Functional-unit results can arrive at different times, while commit keeps architectural state precise and ordered.

content.takeaway: The interesting part is the combination, not any single slogan.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: global pipeline overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ISSUE_STAGE and scoreboard
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The issue path tracks RAW, WAW, and structural hazards, uses forwarding when possible, issues instructions in order, and can still receive out-of-order functional-unit results.

content.speakerNotes: A common beginner mistake is to describe the core with one slogan. In-order. Out-of-order. Single issue. None of those alone is enough. For this CV32A60X path, the useful sentence is: instructions are issued in order, execution results may complete out of order, and commit makes the architectural state retire in order. That sentence will save you from many wrong mental models.

actions.text: A common beginner mistake is to describe the core with one slogan. In-order. Out-of-order. Single issue. None of those alone is enough. For this CV32A60X path, the useful sentence is: instructions are issued in order, execution results may complete out of order, and commit makes the architectural state retire in order. That sentence will save you from many wrong mental models.

scenes.title: Source map for the next five lessons

content.title: Attach each detail to the correct stage
content.subtitle: Unit 03 now walks the spine one segment at a time.

cards.title: FRONTEND
cards.body: PC generation and fetch: how the next instruction enters the machine.

cards.title: ID_STAGE
cards.body: Decode: how instruction bits become scoreboard-ready information.

cards.title: ISSUE / EX_STAGE
cards.body: Hazards, operands, forwarding, and functional-unit execution.

cards.title: COMMIT
cards.body: Precise state update, exceptions, stores, stalls, and flushes.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: FRONTEND module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The FRONTEND module implements PC generation and fetch, requests data from caches, realigns instructions, fills the instruction queue, and transmits instructions to DECODE.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ID_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: ID_STAGE decodes fetched RISC-V instructions, expands compressed instructions, builds scoreboard entries, attaches exceptions when needed, and sends decoded information to ISSUE_STAGE.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: ISSUE_STAGE and scoreboard
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The issue path tracks RAW, WAW, and structural hazards, uses forwarding when possible, issues instructions in order, and can still receive out-of-order functional-unit results.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: COMMIT_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The commit stage updates architectural state, commits stores, writes CSR and register-file state, manages stalling/flushing, and handles precise exceptions at commit.

content.speakerNotes: This is our reading plan. The next lesson reads FRONTEND as PC generation plus fetch. Then we read ID_STAGE as the place where instruction bits become a scoreboard entry. After that, issue and execute explain how hazards and functional units fit together. Finally, commit explains why the machine can be fast internally while still presenting precise architectural state.

actions.text: This is our reading plan. The next lesson reads FRONTEND as PC generation plus fetch. Then we read ID_STAGE as the place where instruction bits become a scoreboard entry. After that, issue and execute explain how hazards and functional units fit together. Finally, commit explains why the machine can be fast internally while still presenting precise architectural state.

content.title: Keep the first architecture story narrow
content.subtitle: A professional introduction avoids importing facts from other configurations or later units.

items.title: Name the configuration
items.body: This path uses CV32A60X design evidence unless another source is named.

items.title: Keep memory details separate
items.body: Memory-system policy is important, but Unit 03 only places it on the spine.

items.title: Do not invent timing
items.body: The document names at least six cycles; detailed timing needs stage-specific evidence.

items.title: Use official terms
items.body: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit are the source-backed labels.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: global pipeline overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

content.speakerNotes: A good architecture introduction is narrow on purpose. We name CV32A60X when that is the source. We do not smuggle in memory-system details before their unit. We do not invent timing beyond what the source supports. And we use the official stage names. This is not being timid. It is how you build trust with technical learners.

actions.text: A good architecture introduction is narrow on purpose. We name CV32A60X when that is the source. We do not smuggle in memory-system details before their unit. We do not invent timing beyond what the source supports. And we use the official stage names. This is not being timid. It is how you build trust with technical learners.

questions.question: Which order matches the official CVA6 six-stage pipeline?

options.label: PC Generation, Fetch, Decode, Issue, Execute, Commit
Fetch, Execute, Decode, Commit, Issue, PC Generation
Decode, PC Generation, Commit, Fetch, Execute, Issue
Issue, Decode, Fetch, PC Generation, Commit, Execute

questions.analysis: The design document names the six-stage order from PC Generation through Commit.

questions.question: Why should a learner start from the architecture block diagram?

options.label: It provides the top-level responsibility map before reading individual RTL files
It replaces the need to read source anchors
It proves every configuration has identical behavior
It is only a decorative image for the course

questions.analysis: The block diagram gives the first map of responsibilities and module names.

questions.question: Which statements are safe for this first architecture lesson?

options.label: CVA6 issues instructions in order on this described path
Results can complete with different backend timing before commit
Every future CVA6 configuration must have the exact same parameter values

questions.analysis: The source supports the ordering model, but parameter values must stay configuration-scoped.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of Unit 03?

options.label: Build a reading spine so later details attach to the right stage
Teach every memory-system corner case immediately

questions.analysis: Unit 03 is a staged reading map, not a replacement for the full documentation.

sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: architecture and modules
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The architecture block diagram groups FRONTEND, DECODE, ISSUE, EXECUTE, COMMIT, CONTROLLER, and CSRFILE as the core reading map.

content.speakerNotes: The quiz checks whether the spine is solid. You should know the stage order, why the official diagram matters, and why the ordering model needs more than one word. If those basics are stable, the later lessons can become more technical without turning into a maze.

actions.text: The quiz checks whether the spine is solid. You should know the stage order, why the official diagram matters, and why the ordering model needs more than one word. If those basics are stable, the later lessons can become more technical without turning into a maze.

content.title: You now have the CVA6 architecture spine
content.subtitle: The next lessons walk the trunk one segment at a time.
takeaways: The official six-stage order is PC Generation, Fetch, Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.
The architecture diagram is the source-backed map for module responsibilities.
A useful CVA6 mental model combines in-order issue, variable backend completion, and in-order commit.
content.nextStep: Next: PC generation to fetch, where the next instruction is chosen and delivered.
content.closing: If the trunk is clean, the branches become much less scary.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: global pipeline overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X design document states that CVA6 implements a six-stage pipeline: PC Generation, Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Issue, Execute, and Commit.

content.speakerNotes: The trunk is ready. You have the official stage order, the high-level diagram, and the ordering sentence that prevents lazy explanations. Next, we zoom into the FRONTEND. The key question will be: where does the next instruction come from, and how does fetch turn that decision into something decode can consume?
